paper.title: In silico cancer immunotherapy trials uncover the consequences of therapy-specific response patterns for clinical trial design and outcome

proof_flows.name: Implement the mechanistic tumor-immune models and baseline parameterization used to generate patient-level tumor trajectories.

items.verify: The reproduction generated a non-empty overview artifact for the mechanistic modeling workflow.
items.expected: A usable artifact exists that shows model components, fitting to survival data, patient simulation, randomized trial construction, and downstream trial-design applications.

items.verify: The model-parameter inventory matches the paper.
items.expected: Table 1 lists four parameters shared by all models (rho, delta, xi, g); total parameter counts are 9 for M1 and 8 each for M2 and M3.

items.verify: The fixed baseline parameter values for the three models match the paper exactly.
The patient-level simulation thresholds used for the trajectory plots match the paper.
items.expected: Each simulation plot starts when tumor burden reaches 10^8 cells, and treatment starts at tumor size 65 x 10^8 cells.

items.verify: The simulated trajectories show the same qualitative response regimes as the paper.
items.expected: Untreated tumors progress to death, treatment can induce partial or complete responses, and the exhaustion-based model shows oscillatory recurrence after apparent clearance.

proof_flows.name: Fit the models to lung cancer and melanoma survival datasets and show that immunotherapy-like survival shapes emerge.

items.verify: The reproduction generated fitted survival-curve artifacts for the reference clinical cohorts.
items.expected: Usable reconstructed survival data and fitted survival-curve artifacts are produced for the reference cohorts.

items.verify: The reconstructed cohort sizes match the figure annotations.
items.expected: The Fig. 3A risk tables start with 228 patients for NCCTG, 250 and 252 for CA184-024 (Ipi+DTIC and DTIC), and 210 and 208 for CheckMate 066 (Nivo and DTIC); these values are shown in the figure's 'No. at risk' rows.

items.verify: The digitized Kaplan-Meier reconstructions visually agree with the source trials.
items.paper_reference: Fig.3A; Results, In silico late-stage immunotherapy trials yield realistic survival outcomes
items.expected: The reconstructed Kaplan-Meier curves and risk tables for the melanoma trials should closely match the published source curves rather than showing obvious mismatch.

items.verify: The fitted in silico trials reproduce the key immunotherapy survival-curve patterns and relative fit quality reported in the paper.
items.paper_reference: Fig.3B; Results, In silico late-stage immunotherapy trials yield realistic survival outcomes
items.expected: Fitted simulations reproduce immunotherapy-like delayed arm separation and later treatment-arm flattening; M1 fits all datasets best, whereas M2 and M3 show visibly poorer fit on at least one dataset.

proof_flows.name: Predict regimen-specific survival and hazard patterns, including violations of proportional hazards, from the fitted models.

items.verify: The reproduction generated survival, hazard, and hazard-ratio outputs for the alternative treatment-regimen scenarios.
items.expected: A usable artifact exists for the regimen-specific survival curves together with accompanying hazard or hazard-ratio summaries.

items.verify: The simulated trial size used for the regimen-comparison scenarios matches the paper.
items.expected: Each Fig. 4 scenario is a 1:1 randomized trial with 600 simulated patients per arm.

items.verify: The reproduced survival patterns match the paper's qualitative claims across treatment scenarios.
items.expected: Transient chemotherapy produces early separation followed by convergence in some models, immunotherapy-containing regimens show delayed separation and non-constant hazards, one scenario yields crossing curves, and another yields only temporary separation.

proof_flows.name: Demonstrate that the preferred treatment-effect metric depends on survival-curve shape and fitted parameterization.

items.verify: The reproduction generated power-analysis outputs comparing hazard-ratio and milestone-survival analyses.
items.expected: A non-empty artifact reports fitted parameter values and the resulting power curves for hazard-ratio and milestone-survival analyses.

items.verify: The fitted parameter values used for later power and interim-analysis figures match the paper exactly.
items.expected: Table 3 values are: M1 log10 rho mean=2.54, log10 rho sd=1.01, chemotherapy effect=0.6, immunotherapy effect=12.15; M2 log10 rho mean=-3.94, log10 rho sd=1.29, chemotherapy effect=0.6, immunotherapy effect=218.75; M3 log10 rho mean=-3.49, log10 rho sd=0.37, chemotherapy effect=0.75, immunotherapy effect=2.25.

items.verify: The simulation setup for the Fig. 5 power curves matches the paper.
items.expected: Figure 5 uses 250 simulated trials per point, and the M3 chemotherapy setting is a 20% reduction in tumor growth rate.

items.verify: The power ordering in the transient chemotherapy-versus-placebo scenario matches the paper.
items.expected: For transient chemotherapy versus placebo, hazard-ratio analysis is more powerful than the milestone survival endpoint across the sampled arm sizes.

items.verify: The endpoint preference flips appropriately across the other response-pattern scenarios.
items.expected: For chemoimmunotherapy versus chemotherapy, the milestone survival endpoint outperforms the hazard ratio, whereas in the long-lasting chemotherapy scenario both analyses improve and the hazard ratio still has an advantage.

proof_flows.name: Use in silico trials to choose analysis endpoint and randomization ratio for chemotherapy and immunotherapy designs.

items.verify: The reproduction generated power curves as a function of trial duration and randomization ratio.
items.expected: A usable artifact exists for power analyses that vary analysis time and allocation ratio.

items.verify: The simulation settings for the endpoint and randomization analyses match the paper.
items.expected: Figure 6 uses 400 simulated trials per point; the randomization-ratio panel uses study size 300 patients in the chemotherapy example and 1200 patients in the immunotherapy example.

items.verify: The optimal endpoint identified for the classical chemotherapy scenario matches the paper.
items.paper_reference: Fig.6A; Results, In silico trials can help to choose endpoints and randomization ratios
items.expected: For the classical chemotherapy scenario, the endpoint with the highest power is 6-month overall survival.

items.verify: The relative importance of endpoint timing versus allocation ratio matches the paper in both therapy settings.
items.expected: Chemotherapy reaches peak power earlier, immunotherapy requires later analysis because of delayed separation, and changing allocation ratio has comparatively little effect on power.

proof_flows.name: Assess how the number and timing of interim analyses trade early stopping against final power.

items.verify: The reproduction generated interim-analysis outcome summaries over time.
items.expected: A non-empty artifact summarizes trial outcomes over time under different interim-analysis plans.

items.verify: The interim-analysis simulation setup matches the paper.
items.expected: The interim-analysis simulations use 1000 trials per scenario, 1200 patients per trial, 1:1 randomization, and treatment-effect settings of strong=12, weak=4, and no effect=0 for the fold increase of T-cell killing.

items.verify: The early-stopping benefit for a strong immunotherapy effect matches the paper.
items.expected: With a strong immunotherapy effect, an interim analysis at 16 months yields about 25% early positive stopping and an analysis at 18 months yields about 40% early positive stopping.

items.verify: The mild loss of final power from adding multiple interim analyses in the weak-effect scenario matches the paper.
items.paper_reference: Fig.7B; Results, Simulating the effects of interim analyses
items.expected: With a weak immunotherapy effect, approximately 88% of trials are positive with no interim analysis and approximately 86% are positive with three equally spaced interim analyses.

items.verify: The no-effect control scenario preserves the expected false-positive behavior.
items.expected: In the no-effect control scenario, about 95% of trials end negative regardless of the number of interim analyses.
